title: How Societies Can Succeed in the AI Era
gist: RAND senior political scientist Michael Mazarr argues that national success in the AI era hinges on social characteristics, not just chips, models, and data centers, and that the U.S. is having almost no dialogue about the choices needed to make AI enhance citizen agency rather than strip it, with education identified as the central lever and AI-driven social fragmentation as the biggest risk.

insights.claim: Mazarr characterizes AI as potentially the most wide-ranging and influential general-purpose technology in human history, with applications across nearly every social, economic, and political activity simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: If AI's breadth exceeds prior GPTs, the addressable transformation spans every sector, not a few; supports broad rather than narrow AI exposure.
insights.quote: potentially the most wide-ranging and influential general purpose technology in human history
insights.locator: In the paper that we're going to be discussing

insights.claim: Mazarr contrasts AI with electricity, noting electricity revolutionized some industries but not others (e.g. education), whereas AI has applications across the board including manufacturing, universities, and medicine.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: The cross-sector simultaneity is what makes AI's disruption faster and harder to manage than past GPTs; informs the pace of incumbent disruption.
insights.quote: electricity had a dramatic effect on society in many ways, but it did not necessarily revolutionize the practice of education
insights.locator: I mean, when we think about historical parallels

insights.claim: Mazarr's core thesis is that the AI competitive challenge is primarily social rather than technological: the countries that acquire a technology are not necessarily the ones that prosper most 50 years later.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI race away from chip/model leadership toward societal absorption capacity; a different lens for assessing national winners.
insights.quote: the competitive challenge is primarily social, not technological
insights.locator: But your core thesis is that the competitive challenge

insights.claim: Mazarr argues France had scientists pushing industrial-era innovation as much as Britain, but Britain led the Industrial Revolution because of social characteristics: amenability to entrepreneurs, private-sector capital reservoirs, and networks of engineers who experimented with new technologies.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Historical precedent that capital availability and entrepreneurial networks, not raw science, decide who captures a GPT's value.
insights.quote: it did not become the world leader of the Industrial Revolution in the way that Britain did because of a number of characteristics of British society
insights.locator: France had scientists

insights.claim: Mazarr notes Britain needed 50 to 75 years of reforms (child labor, labor standards, workers' rights, environmental and political reforms) to shape the Industrial Revolution's effects, implying AI requires comparable adaptive reform.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: The reform timeline that made past GPTs socially sustainable spans decades, but Mazarr argues AI does not allow that much time.
insights.quote: Britain had to go through a period of 50 or 75 years of reforms to shape the effect of the technology
insights.locator: And then eventually, Britain had to go through

insights.claim: Mazarr cites the Cold War as evidence that despite Soviet basic-science leads after Sputnik, the more dynamic, innovative, open, and adaptable nature of American and allied societies determined the eventual outcome.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Reinforces that societal adaptability beats early technical leads; relevant to handicapping U.S.-China AI competition.
insights.quote: it was the nature of American and allied societies, more dynamic, more innovative, more open, more adaptable, that meant that they prevailed
insights.locator: And another great historical example

insights.claim: RAND work for the Office of Net Assessment on long-term U.S.-China competition identified seven societal qualities that produce competitive outputs like innovation and military power, treating those outputs as outcomes rather than root causes.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Provides a diagnostic checklist for assessing which nations will convert AI into durable advantage.
insights.quote: we think of as sort of outputs or outcomes. And we looked at the qualities of societies that tend to produce those things
insights.locator: Yeah, so again, in this work we did

insights.claim: The first identified quality is national ambition and willpower: a society-wide sense of destiny spanning political leadership, population, business, science, and culture, with many ambitious people seeking to shape the world.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Ambition is a measurable national-competitiveness input that AI can either fuel or drain.
insights.quote: the first one is national ambition and willpower
insights.locator: And we did a lot of research

insights.claim: A second quality is shared opportunity: societies that let more people express their talent are stronger than those keeping segments under wraps through bias, discrimination, or lack of economic opportunity.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Inclusion is framed as a competitiveness driver, not just an equity goal; AI's effect on mobility becomes a strategic variable.
insights.quote: the more people in society that have more opportunity to express their potential, their talent, their capacity, the more strength a society is going to have
insights.locator: Another one is shared opportunity

insights.claim: A third quality, Mazarr's favorite, is a learning and adapting society: one with intellectual energy across officials, scientists, business and military leaders who push the frontiers of knowledge and then change their institutions based on what they learn.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Institutional adaptability is the trait most directly tested by AI's rapid pace; a key differentiator among great powers.
insights.quote: We called it a learning and adapting society.
insights.locator: And another one that is sort of my favorite

insights.claim: Mazarr warns of cognitive offloading, where people lose ambition and come to rely on AI to do their thinking, potentially empowering a small elite while draining the larger society of ambition and willpower.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: A society can grow more powerful by some measures while hollowing out its human-capital base; a hidden downside of AI adoption.
insights.quote: there's a way that AI could empower a very small number of people in society ... while draining the larger society of ambition and willpower
insights.locator: And people ... there's evidence that people can come to rely

insights.claim: Mazarr's fundamental theme is that outcomes depend on the choices made to render AI empowering and increase citizen agency, versus stealing agency and transferring it to an abstract entity.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Positions deliberate policy/design choices, not the technology itself, as the decisive variable for societal outcomes.
insights.quote: it's all about the choices we make to make this technology empowering and increase the agency of citizens
insights.locator: And that gets to sort of a fundamental theme

insights.claim: Mazarr says the U.S. is having almost zero dialogue about the choices needed to ensure Americans feel their agency has been enhanced by AI.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: A flagged policy vacuum; the absence of this dialogue is itself the risk Mazarr is most concerned about.
insights.quote: We are having almost zero dialogue about the choices we need to make
insights.locator: And that's one of the things that concerns me

insights.claim: Mazarr cites DeepSeek, China's open-source model that became competitive with leading American frontier models, as triggering an outpouring of nationalist pride within China and validating its scientific-advance ambitions.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Demonstrates how an AI milestone can fuel the 'national ambition' competitiveness quality; a soft-power dimension of model leadership.
insights.quote: there was kind of an outpouring of nationalist pride
insights.locator: An interesting case of that is when DeepSeek came out

insights.claim: Mazarr argues AI can act as a co-pilot that empowers individuals against large institutions, for example an AI model that helps a citizen contest a local government agency by demonstrating legal principles.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete pro-agency use case; legal/administrative AI assistance could rebalance citizen-institution power.
insights.quote: you are empowered in the face of larger institutions as opposed to disempowered
insights.locator: For example, if you've got an AI model

insights.claim: Mazarr argues the socially empowering response to AI is not dictated from the top but must be grassroots, multidimensional, and pluralistic, led mainly by state and local governments, philanthropies, individuals, and private-sector firms rather than the federal government.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Shifts the locus of AI governance action away from federal law toward distributed actors; informs where policy momentum will form.
insights.quote: the answer to that in the way that makes it socially empowering is not dictated from the top
insights.locator: One thing I would say is, it's not about Washington

insights.claim: Mazarr notes the British Industrial Revolution was shaped from the 1830s to century's end by reform movements combining popular activists, reform-minded elites, and prominent cultural figures, a model he applies to AI adaptation.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Historical template for the coalition structure Mazarr expects to drive AI-era reform.
insights.quote: you had a whole series of reform movements across a variety of issues that helped to shape the Industrial Revolution
insights.locator: Like in the British case

insights.claim: Mazarr stresses the AI adaptation process should be experimentation rather than putting final answers in place, but warns the multi-decade timeline available for past technologies does not exist for AI.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Compressed timeline plus experimentation-first approach defines the realistic policy posture; speed is the binding constraint.
insights.quote: We don't have that much time is part of the problem with AI.
insights.locator: So if this were Industrial Revolution

themes.theme: Social fragmentation as the top risk

insights.claim: Mazarr identifies AI's potential to intensify social divisions, fragmentation, and distrust as one of the biggest risk areas, because AI is arriving onto an already unstable social situation rather than a calm one.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Frames societal instability, not loss-of-control alignment failure, as the most likely near-term harm; a macro-social risk to watch.
insights.quote: this has the potential of really intensifying social divisions, social fragmentation, lack of trust that's already so rife in our society
insights.locator: And so to answer your question, to me, the risk is

insights.claim: Mazarr argues AI is more mysterious to ordinary people than a railroad engine was in the Industrial Revolution, functioning as a complete black box that breeds alienation and a sense of threat from job loss.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Opacity plus job fear drives the alienation that fuels populist backlash; an adoption-resistance and political-risk factor.
insights.quote: how it works, how it's being applied, what the future looks like, is a complete black box to many people
insights.locator: But in this context, I think the risks

insights.claim: Mazarr notes some California universities trying to mandate AI use to make students comfortable have hit resistance from students who object on resource grounds (electricity, water) and refuse to be forced to use it.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Mandated-adoption backlash signals friction for AI rollouts in education; environmental concerns are a live objection vector.
insights.quote: You can't mandate this on me. I'm opposed to the use of it.
insights.locator: So some processes that have begun, for example, in California

insights.claim: Mazarr links AI-driven loss-of-control sentiment to populist movements, citing Brexit's 'take back control' motto as the recurring theme AI could amplify by an order of magnitude.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: AI could supercharge the loss-of-control grievance underlying populist politics; a second-order political-risk channel.
insights.quote: The motto of the Brexit movement in Britain was take back control.
insights.locator: I mean, this is, you know, if you look back at like Brexit

insights.claim: Mazarr references Anthropic recently warning about AI risks including loss of control and misalignment as well as misuse through cyber attacks and biological attacks.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Frontier-lab safety warnings are entering policy discourse; the misuse vectors (cyber, bio) are distinct from the social-fragmentation risk Mazarr emphasizes.
insights.quote: which Anthropic recently warned about—not only about that risk, but risks of misuse with cyber attacks, biological attacks
insights.locator: I mean, on the one hand, you've got risks of loss of control

insights.claim: Mazarr says basic AI literacy, a fundamental understanding of what these systems are and how they work, is consistently among the first recommendations for addressing AI's social problems, while admitting he still cannot fully explain aspects of transformer-based LLMs after two and a half years.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: AI literacy programs are a likely funding and policy priority; the technology's irreducible complexity is itself a barrier.
insights.quote: there's a phrase that's often used of AI literacy
insights.locator: So absolutely, yes. And that is very often

insights.claim: Mazarr points to the latest executive order introducing some government pre-evaluation of models before release as part of a needed public-private dialogue signaling that governments are protecting citizens' interests.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Pre-release model evaluation is an emerging regulatory mechanism; relevant to compliance burden on frontier developers.
insights.quote: some degree of government pre-evaluation of models before they are released
insights.locator: A second part of the solution has to be a public-private dialogue

insights.claim: Mazarr flags the persistent trade-off in AI oversight: going too far in government evaluation risks constraining innovation, though he believes a decent balance can theoretically be struck.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: The regulation-versus-innovation tension defines the policy design space; outcomes affect developer freedom and pace.
insights.quote: there's a fear that if you go too far in that direction, you're going to constrain innovation
insights.locator: There's always a trade-off here

insights.claim: Mazarr states China, per central-government directives, is moving faster than the U.S. on integrating AI awareness into schooling at all levels, though implementation extent is unknown.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: A claimed U.S.-China gap in AI education directives; a competitiveness indicator to track on the 'learning society' axis.
insights.quote: China, in terms of the directives of the central government, is moving faster than us on this.
insights.locator: China, in terms of the directives

insights.claim: Mazarr nominates education as the single issue ripe for initial action across all levels of governance, recommending the next two to five years be treated as a period of principled experimentation rather than a search for one final answer.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Education AI policy is the highest-priority experimentation arena; watch state/university pilots over a 2-5 year horizon.
insights.quote: how do we envision the next two to five years as a period of experimentation
insights.locator: It's how do we envision the next two to five years

insights.claim: Mazarr concludes education sits at the hub of the other competitiveness issues because an informed populace expresses more ambition, has more opportunity, and is more ready for learning, summarizing the stakes as education and power.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Education is positioned as the multiplier on all other societal competitiveness traits in the AI era.
insights.quote: education and power, two words that really reflect what's at stake
insights.locator: So, those kind of power relationships

insights.claim: Mazarr argues AI has the potential to tilt power relationships between people and major institutions (the IRS, large corporations, the legal system) either in favor of citizens or further against them, making those power dynamics a defining stake.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Citizen-versus-institution power rebalancing is a key design choice with broad societal and political consequences.
insights.quote: AI has the potential to tilt those relationships in favor of citizens ... or tilt it in the other way
insights.locator: I do think one other huge issue

insights.claim: Mazarr's individual-action prescription is threefold: educate yourself on AI basics, become familiar with the models by using them for everyday queries (not advanced agentic uses), and learn what your public officials advocate on AI.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: Concrete grassroots playbook; widespread hands-on model use is part of building the informed populace Mazarr argues competitiveness requires.
insights.quote: Yeah, well, I would say three things to start with. One is educate yourself
insights.locator: Yeah, well, I would say three things

insights.claim: Mazarr warns that if Americans come to believe elected officials do not have their interests at heart on AI, it further undermines the perceived legitimacy of governing institutions already under threat.
insights.speaker: Michael Mazarr
insights.so_what: AI mishandling is framed as an accelerant of institutional-legitimacy decline; a systemic political-stability risk.
insights.quote: if the American people come to believe that their elected officials don't have their interests at heart, that further undermines the perceived legitimacy
insights.locator: That's another kind of worry of mine
